retrieved_docs.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Motor Neuron Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: A combination of dextromethorphan and quinidine sulfate has been approved for pseudobulbar effect. It is contraindicated in patients using monoamine oxidase inhibitors, and caution should be taken with patients on selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors due to the risk of serotonin syndrome. [3] Edaravone, a free radical scavenger, is a recently approved drug that decreases oxidative stress, contributing to neuron cell death. It has been shown in some clinical trials to slow the functional decline in ALS patients. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Pseudobulbar affect is commonly seen in PLS, and recognition with screening and early treatment with medications can reduce social withdrawal and positively impact patients' quality of life. Treatment with dextromethorphan/quinidine is commonly used. In patients who cannot tolerate dextromethorphan/quinidine or do not benefit from treatment, amitriptyline or fluvoxamine can be trialed. However, neither has been studied in controlled trials for the pseudobulbar effect. [1] Screening for mood symptoms, especially depression, is essential, and early referral to a psychiatrist or therapist is recommended when appropriate. Riluzole, a Food and Drug Administration-approved drug for ALS that provides a modest survival benefit of a few months, has not shown survival benefits in patients with PLS and is currently not recommended in the treatment of PLS. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Riluzole, Edaravone, and sodium phenylbutyrate are the only approved medications to treat ALS but provide limited survival benefits. [1] [3] Riluzole, 50mg bid, is recommended for all patients with ALS and is thought to reduce glutamate-induced excitotoxicity. Riluzole is the only medication that improves overall survival (74% vs. 58% in the placebo group at 12 months) and slows the progression of symptoms (ie, survival without tracheostomy (57% vs. 50% at 18 months). Riluzole is most effective in patients who have been symptomatic for less than 5 years, have a vital capacity (VC) >60% of the predicted value, and do not require a tracheostomy. [36] It is important to note that the elimination of riluzole will be affected by CYP1A2 inhibitors like caffeine and theophylline (decrease the rate of elimination of riluzole). Transaminase elevation occurs in approximately 50% of patients. Monitor transaminases monthly for the first three months and every three months after.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: AVP-923 (dextromethorphan-quinidine 20mg/10mg) has proven effective in patients with pseudobulbar affect. [45] Less rigorous evidence has supported the use of amitriptyline and fluvoxamine in smaller trials. Pain is treated based on its underlying cause. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medications, paracetamol, intra-articular steroids, or lidocaine injections are recommended for nociceptive pain, whereas gabapentin, pregabalin, tricyclic antidepressants, and morphine are advised for neuropathic pain. Mexiletine, levetiracetam, and quinine sulfate help patients with cramps. Assistive devices such as special mattresses, pillows, and wheelchairs may help to prevent pain. Ultimately, many patients require nonopioid analgesics and anti-inflammatory medications, and when these fail, opioids become the mainstay of pain treatment. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate the etiology of ALS. Identify the typical findings on the examination of a patient with ALS. Determine the interventions available for patients with ALS. Apply effective strategies to improve care coordination among interprofessional team members to facilitate positive outcomes for patients with ALS. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium phenylbutyrate is a histone deacetylase inhibitor that decreases the stress response in the endoplasmic reticulum. Combined with taurursodiol (which increases the threshold for cellular apoptosis), it decreases neuronal destruction. The result is a slower decline in function. Sodium phenylbutyrate-taurursodiol is administered intrathecally and may result in myelitis, radiculitis, aseptic meningitis, and elevated intracranial pressure.

retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary spastic paraplegia Adrenomyeloneuropathy Late-onset Tay-Sachs disease Polyglucosan body disease HIV myelopathy Multiple sclerosis Myasthenia gravis Nutritional myeloneuropathies Post-polio syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This topic will review the etiology, diagnosis, and evaluation of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). In addition, it outlines the treatments and interventions available. This topic highlights the role of the interprofessional healthcare team in evaluating and treating patients with ALS.

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Neuron Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Palliative Medicine Interventions
retrieved_docs.content: Reduction in ICU utilization at end of life, the number of deaths inside the hospital setting (transition in the place of care), and costs of treatment are essential indicators of the efficacy of provision of high-quality care. [89] For patients being treated in the intensive care setting, discussions centering upon withdrawal of life-sustaining measures and DNCPR (Do not perform cardiopulmonary resuscitation) decisions may be indicated. [90]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Arrest -- Treatment / Management -- Physician
retrieved_docs.content: Providers can use ALS treatment and progress to a wide scope of practice dependent on medical versus traumatic etiology.

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinergics are used to manage excessive secretions similar to ALS. Examples include glycopyrrolate, scopolamine, or atropine drops. In select patients who do not respond to oral medications, botulinum toxin injection into the submandibular gland can be helpful. Cognitive and neuropsychological features can be seen in PLS, and cognitive screening tests should be implemented in patients with PLS to assess for possible frontotemporal dysfunction, language deficits, and problems with executive function. [3] Formal neuropsychological testing should be done in patients who perform poorly on these cognitive screening tests.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Frequent and painful muscle spasms are common. Mexiletine (a sodium channel-blocking antiarrhythmic drug) is effective at a 150 mg BID dose. [40] [41] Other options include levetiracetam, gabapentin, baclofen, and tizanidine. [42] When oral therapy is impractical, botulinum toxin injections into the spastic muscles can be helpful. As weakness and functional decline inevitably progress, patients use assistive devices (canes, orthoses, crutches, and eventually wheelchairs), removable headrests in those with neck weakness, specialized utensils and holders, and eventually a pressure-relieving mattress with frequent repositioning to prevent pressure ulcers. [43] Sialorrhea is very common and can be treated with atropine, hyoscyamine, amitriptyline, glycopyrrolate, botulinum toxin injections into salivary glands, and even low-dose radiation therapy in those with refractory symptoms. [35] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Diaphragmatic Pacing -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Controversy surrounds the use of DP in patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). One study showed improved survival by delaying the need for mechanical ventilation. [11] The authors of this trial did a long-term follow up of these patients and confirmed their original findings. [12] However, there are two trials that have shown decreased survival in these patients. [13] [14] Until more conclusive evidence is available showing clear benefit, we suggest weighing the risks and benefits of the procedure with a team in a specialized center. DP should not be done in patients who do have phrenic nerve function as determined by nerve conduction studies.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: One classic presentation of ALS may not exist. Personalized therapy needs to be implemented. [3] Each patient must be evaluated for ALS mimics. Treatment at multidisciplinary ALS clinics improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Afferent Loop Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity reviews the etiology, evaluation, and management of afferent loop syndrome (ALS), with particular attention to the difference in management between benign and malignant etiologies. This activity highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the diagnosis and expedient treatment of patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: There is no cure for ALS. [5] [7] The median survival is 3 to 5 years. Approximately 30% of patients survive 5 years and 10% ten years. Riluzole provides a survival benefit averaging 3 months, with edaravone showing a 33% reduction in the decline of the ALS Functional Rating Scale–Revised (ALSFRS-R) at 6 months.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1376
retrieved_docs.content: Inotropic agents, antiparkinsonika, and radiofarmaka are all classified as medicinal products that are defined by their therapeutic role. Inotropic agents are substances used to enhance the contractile strength of the heart muscle, representing a therapeutic category of drugs aimed at improving cardiac output. Antiparkinsonika comprise a therapeutic group of medicines designed to alleviate the motor and non‑motor symptoms of Parkinson’s disease, thereby forming another distinct subclass within the broader category of therapeutically defined medicinal products. Radiofarmaka, also known as radiopharmaceuticals, are agents that contain radioactive isotopes and are employed for diagnostic imaging or targeted therapy, and they likewise belong to the overarching classification of medicinal products organized by therapeutic purpose. Together, these three groups illustrate how diverse drug classes—ranging from cardiac inotropes to neuro‑protective agents and radiolabeled compounds—are unified under the concept of therapeutic role–based categorization in pharmacology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2789
retrieved_docs.content: Deslanosid is a pharmacologic agent that may be employed in the management of decreased cardiac output, indicating its potential therapeutic role in conditions where the heart’s pumping ability is compromised.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2827
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamine and ambenonium chloride are both classified as parasympathomimetic agents, meaning they mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system by stimulating cholinergic receptors and enhancing parasympathetic tone. These drugs are used in clinical contexts where increased cholinergic activity is beneficial, such as in the treatment of certain neurological or autonomic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1547
retrieved_docs.content: Inotropic agent (substance) is classified as a medicinal product categorized by therapeutic role, indicating that it belongs to the group of drugs defined by their intended therapeutic effect. Antiparkinsonika, likewise, is a subclass of medicinal products categorized by therapeutic role, representing agents used specifically to treat Parkinson’s disease. Both inotropic agents and antiparkinsonika are organized under the broader classification of medicinal products based on their therapeutic roles, highlighting the systematic approach to grouping drugs according to their clinical purpose.

retrieved_docs.content: cardiac contractility) and antiparkinsonika (drugs used in Parkinson’s disease) are both classified as medicinal products by therapeutic role, underscoring the broader pharmacological framework that groups drugs according to their intended clinical effect. Overall, the hierarchy demonstrates that VASOPROTECTIVES encompass antivaricose agents, capillary stabilizing agents, and specific chemical entities, all of which contribute to vascular health, while the therapeutic‑role classification system situates these agents alongside other pharmacological classes such as inotropes and antiparkinsonian drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1374
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and bethanechol are both classified as parasympathomimetics, a group of agents that mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. By stimulating cholinergic pathways, these drugs enhance parasympathetic tone, which can be therapeutically useful in conditions where increased muscarinic activity is desired. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action despite being used for different clinical indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1432
retrieved_docs.content: Amrinon and deslanosid are both classified within the group of cardiotonic agents (kardiotonika). As members of this pharmacologic class, they are utilized in the therapeutic management of conditions characterized by reduced cardiac output (انخفاض النتاج القلبي). Their use aims to enhance myocardial contractility and improve overall cardiac performance in patients experiencing low-output states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2145
retrieved_docs.content: Bromodiphenhydramine and pimavanserin are pharmacologic agents that may be employed in the treatment of Parkinson’s disease. Bromodiphenhydramine, an antihistamine with anticholinergic activity, has potential utility for alleviating motor symptoms associated with the neurodegenerative disorder. Pimavanserin, a selective serotonin‑5‑HT₂A inverse agonist, is also recognized for its possible therapeutic role in Parkinson’s disease, particularly for managing psychotic features and may provide broader symptomatic benefit. Recognizing both drugs as possible treatment options expands therapeutic considerations for clinicians managing patients with Parkinson’s disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1999
retrieved_docs.content: Amrinon and deslanosid are classified as kardiotonika, a group of cardiotonic agents that are employed to improve low cardiac output (انخفاض النتاج القلبي). Both drugs therefore may be used to treat conditions characterized by insufficient cardiac performance. However, the use of deslanosid, as well as digitoxin, is contraindicated in patients who have experienced cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, indicating that these cardiotonic agents should be avoided in the setting of such life‑threatening arrhythmias. Careful assessment of the patient’s cardiac rhythm and output is essential when selecting a kardiotonika for therapy.

The class is also linked to the neuropeptide galanin, suggesting possible pharmacological interactions. Clinical caution is required because homatropine methylbromide and butorfanol are contraindicated in patients with respiratory depression, highlighting the need for careful assessment when anticholinergic agents affect respiratory function. Finally, CGRP receptor antagonists are mentioned as another receptor‑antagonist class relevant to migraine treatment, illustrating the wider context of receptor‑blocking therapeutics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2911
retrieved_docs.content: 4‑Aminopyridine is a pharmacologic agent that may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). By blocking voltage‑gated potassium channels, 4‑aminopyridine can improve nerve conduction and enhance motor function in patients with multiple sclerosis, thereby serving as a therapeutic option to alleviate some of the disease’s neurological symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and edrofonium are both classified as parasympathomimetic agents, meaning they mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. As members of this pharmacologic class, they enhance cholinergic transmission and can be used to support functions mediated by acetylcholine, such as cognition, memory, and certain autonomic processes. Recognizing both galantamin and edrofonium as parasympathomimetics helps clinicians understand their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications in conditions where augmentation of parasympathetic activity is desired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 154
retrieved_docs.content: central nervous system depressant effects. Mesuprine hydrochloride possesses beta‑adrenergic receptor agonist activity, indicating a role in stimulating adrenergic pathways beyond muscle relaxation. Finally, mefenesin is a child entity of propylene glycol, reflecting a formulation or solvent relationship rather than a direct therapeutic action. This structured taxonomy aids clinicians in understanding chemical families, mechanisms of action (peripheral versus direct), and the range of individual drug options available for neuromuscular and neurological patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1451
retrieved_docs.content: Anticholinergic agents constitute a pharmacologic class that blocks acetylcholine receptors, and within this class the specific drugs profenamine, benzatropine‑methanesulfonate, biperiden, biperiden lactate, and trihexyfenidyl are recognized as child compounds. All of these agents share the anticholinergic mechanism of action and are employed in clinical practice for conditions such as Parkinsonian symptoms and drug‑induced extrapyramidal side effects. Understanding that profenamine, benzatropine‑methanesulfonate, biperiden, biperiden lactate, and trihexyfenidyl belong to the broader anticholinergic category aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy and anticipating class‑related adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: from this chemical class. Butorphanol, like homatropine methylbromide, is contraindicated in patients with respiratory depression. Myorelaxancia represents a broad class of neuromuscular agents linked to nervosvalové látky (neuromuscular substances) and includes neuromuscular blockers, depolarizing agents, and the classic muscle relaxant curare. Autonomní látky are described as a child of myorelaxancia, highlighting bidirectional relationships among autonomic and muscle‑relaxant categories. Collectively, these interwoven hierarchies demonstrate how agents can belong simultaneously to multiple mechanistic, therapeutic, and chemical groups—linking receptor antagonism, calcium‑channel blockade, dopamine or adrenergic inhibition, vasodilation, antitussive and pro‑kinetic effects, and safety considerations—to support nuanced pharmacotherapy and patient‑centered care.
